mlsynth 0.1 documentation

Get started

  • About mlsynth
  • A practitioner’s decision tree
  • Replications
    • FDID — Forward Difference-in-Differences (Li 2024)
    • TSSC — Two-Step Synthetic Control (Li & Shankar 2024)
    • VanillaSC — Standard Synthetic Control (ADH 2010/2015; Abadie-Gardeazabal 2003)
    • Ridge ASCM — Augmented Synthetic Control (Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein 2021)
    • SparseSC — L1 Predictor Selection for Synthetic Controls (Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • NSC — Nonlinear Synthetic Control (Tian 2023)
    • SDID — Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021)
    • MCNNM — Matrix Completion with Nuclear-Norm Minimization (Athey et al. 2021)
    • SpSyDiD — Spatial Synthetic-DiD (Serenini & Masek 2024)
    • SEQ_SDID — Sequential Synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky & Samkov 2025)
    • ClusterSC — Synthetic Control with Donor Selection (Rho et al. 2025)
    • LEXSCM — Synthetic Experimental Design (Abadie-Zhao 2026; Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • SCMO — Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (Tian et al. 2026; Sun et al. 2025)
    • RESCM — SCM-relaxation (Liao, Shi & Zheng 2026)
    • LINF / L1LINF — L-infinity-norm SC (Wang, Xing & Ye 2025)
    • GEOLIFT — Meta’s GeoLift walkthrough (augsynth cross-validation)
    • ROLLDID — Lee & Wooldridge rolling-transformation DiD
    • PPSCM — augsynth multisynth (Paglayan collective bargaining)
    • SNN — deshen24/syntheticNN (Prop 99)
    • SYNDES — Doudchenko et al. (2021) BLS Monte Carlo
    • SPCD — Lu et al. (2022) Prop 99 design study
    • SPOTSYNTH — O’Riordan & Gilligan-Lee (2025) spillover detection
    • MAREX — Abadie & Zhao (2026) Walmart design (independent validator)
    • DSC — Distributional Synthetic Controls on Dube (2019)
    • MicroSynth — panel method vs the R microsynth (Seattle DMI)
  • Benchmarks
  • References

Observational: canonical workhorses

  • Vanilla Synthetic Control (VanillaSC)
  • Two-Step Synthetic Control
  • Forward Difference-in-Differences (FDID)

Observational: decomposition-first

  • Synthetic Business Cycle (SBC)
  • Harmonic Synthetic Control (HSC)

Observational: generalised estimand / treatment / unit

  • Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (SCMO)
  • Continuous-Treatment Synthetic Control (CTSC)
  • Distributional Synthetic Control (DSC)
  • Synthetic Interventions (SI)
  • MicroSynth (User-Level Balancing SC)

Observational: convex-hull relaxation

  • Imperfect Synthetic Controls (ISCM)
  • Nonlinear Synthetic Control (NSC)

Observational: No Donors

  • Synthetic Historical Control (SHC)

Observational: high-dimensional donors

  • Bayesian Synthetic Control with a Soft Simplex Constraint (BVS-SS)
  • Cluster Synthetic Controls (CLUSTERSC)
  • Multi-Level Synthetic Control (mlSC)
  • Forward-Selected Synthetic Control (FSCM)
  • Matching and Synthetic Control (MASC)
  • Multivariate Square-root Lasso Synthetic Control (MSQRT)
  • Sparse Synthetic Control (SparseSC)
  • Panel Data Approach (PDA)
  • Relaxed / Penalized Synthetic Control (RESCM)

Observational: time-aware and factor models

  • Time-Aware Synthetic Control (TASC)
  • Factor Model Approach (FMA)
  • Dynamic Synthetic Control for Auto-Regressive processes (DSCAR)

Observational: staggered adoption

  • Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID)
  • Sequential Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Sequential SDiD)
  • Partially Pooled SCM (PPSCM)
  • Staggered Synthetic Control (SSC)

Observational: spillover-aware

  • Spatial Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SpSyDiD)
  • Spillover-Aware Synthetic Control (SPILLSYNTH)
  • Spillover-Detecting Synthetic Control (SPOTSYNTH)

Observational: missing data

  • Matrix Completion with Nuclear Norm Minimization (MCNNM)
  • Synthetic Nearest Neighbors / Causal Matrix Completion (SNN)
  • Robust Matrix estimation with Side Information (RMSI)

Observational: endogenous treatment

  • Synthetic IV
  • Proximal Inference Synthetic Control (PROXIMAL)

Experimental design

  • Lexicographic Synthetic Control (LEXSCM)
  • Synthetic Controls for Experimental Design (MAREX)
  • Synthetic Design (SYNDES)
  • Parallel-Trends Supergeo Design (PANGEO)
  • Synthetic Principal Component Design (SPCD)
  • Modified Unbiased Synthetic Control
  • Rolling-Transformation DiD (ROLLDID)

Utilities and internals

  • Data Utilities
  • Helper Utilities
  • .rst

Welcome to mlsynth 0.1.2

Welcome to mlsynth 0.1.2#

Synthetic control, for everyone.

mlsynth is an open-source Python toolbox of synthetic-control methods for program evaluation. It implements the classical Abadie-Diamond-Hainmueller estimator alongside a growing catalogue of modern variants – Bayesian spike-and-slab selection, state-space modelling, instrumental variants, sequential difference-in-differences, matrix completion – under a single long-DataFrame API. Every estimator’s documentation page includes a Verification section that reproduces the original paper’s reported numbers where applicable.

For example, the following code replicates Abadie, Diamond and Hainmueller’s Proposition 99 study end-to-end. It loads the panel shipped with the library, fits TSSC (which auto-selects between four SC-class variants based on a pre-trends test), and prints the recommended ATT with a 95% subsampling confidence interval:

import pandas as pd
from mlsynth import TSSC

# Long panel: 50 US states x 31 years of per-capita cigarette sales.
url = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/"
       "main/basedata/prop99_packsales.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(url)
df["treat"] = ((df["state"] == "California")
                & (df["year"] >= 1989)).astype(int)

res = TSSC({"df": df, "outcome": "cigsale", "unitid": "state",
             "time": "year", "treat": "treat",
             "display_graphs": False, "seed": 0}).fit()

print(f"recommended: {res.recommended_method}")
print(f"ATT = {res.att:+.2f} packs/yr  "
       f"(95% CI: {res.att_ci[0]:+.2f}, {res.att_ci[1]:+.2f})")

prints:

recommended: SC
ATT = -14.95 packs/yr  (95% CI: -16.06, -9.65)

This short script is a representative example of what mlsynth can do. In addition to classical SC, mlsynth also supports Bayesian variable selection (Bayesian Synthetic Control with a Soft Simplex Constraint (BVS-SS)), staggered-adoption sequential difference-in-differences (Sequential Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Sequential SDiD), Spatial Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SpSyDiD)), instrumental synthetic control (Synthetic IV), matrix completion under missingness (Matrix Completion with Nuclear Norm Minimization (MCNNM)), state- space time-aware control (Time-Aware Synthetic Control (TASC)), and clustered / robust high- dimensional variants (Cluster Synthetic Controls (CLUSTERSC), Multi-Level Synthetic Control (mlSC), Synthetic Controls for Experimental Design (MAREX)).

For a guided tour of the estimator catalogue, start with the About mlsynth page. Browse the Estimators sidebar for the full list grouped by methodology.

mlsynth builds on top of numpy, pandas, scipy, scikit-learn, cvxpy, pydantic, and statsmodels; convex programs are routed through cvxpy’s solver stack.

Not sure which estimator to use? Walk the A practitioner’s decision tree decision tree – a sequence of identification and design questions that funnels you from “what kind of problem do I have?” down to one or two methods, with the catalogue grouped by family.

Community.

The mlsynth community spans economists, statisticians, and data scientists who use synthetic-control methods for program evaluation across policy, marketing, sports, and public health. We welcome you to join us!

  • To share feature requests and bug reports, use the issue tracker.

  • To follow development, watch the mlsynth repository on GitHub.

Development.

mlsynth is maintained by Jared Greathouse (Georgia State University). The project would not be possible without the kind efforts of and discussions with Jason Coupet, Kathy Li, Mani Bayani, Zhentao Shi, and Jaume Vives-i-Bastida, along with a growing list of contributors.

News.

The verification campaign now covers thirty-two of the thirty-six estimators in mlsynth – each auditing its implementation against its source paper, either by reproducing an empirical Table value on the authors’ own data (“Path A”) or by reproducing a Monte Carlo from the paper’s simulation section (“Path B”), or against an authoritative reference implementation. See the Replications page for the full catalogue with headline numbers.
